item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just realized its basically the firework chair from kung fu panda!

item.title: Science issues
item.content:encoded:
I know that SCP do not need to obey the laws of physics as we know them in any regard whatsoever, and I am okay with that. But the article makes a number of issues with the science put forth. If this was part of the effect of the SCP that would be awesome, but it doesn't seem to be done purposefully enough to make me believe that. Perhaps not only changing the laws of physics but the understanding of physics for anyone nearby would have been quite interesting, alas.

Basically, the chair is treated rather grandly despite the fact that there seems to be little proof of it's anomalous properties. My alternative theory for how it came to be that fits the given evidence.

A pair of rockets was strapped to the chair, lit, and failed to get it anywhere with the person sitting on it, so they replaced them with different ones, but just before testing it again, the object was placed into orbit by some other anomalous object.

A few cheap and easy tests would be, per property.

  1. Cut one of the fuses off (or pull it out) to see if it regenerates
  2. No easy way to test this without possibly losing it again in space, but it could potentially be a cheap way to reach space if proven true, which might be worth the trouble of seeing if a rocket detached from the chair retains it's properties.
  3. Put it in a wind tunnel to see if it resists drag, air should go around/through it without slowing, or impart no force on it even if it does
  4. This property is weird in my opinion. Just stopping after you get to space (in relation to earth anyway) in is a very quick way to get back to the surface. Stopping after getting to orbit doesn't make much sense, unless you just suddenly jump to orbital speed. If you were going quite a bit faster than orbital speed, this might be considered a stop, but is confusing. In any case, the occupant of the chair would likely be thrown clean of the chair if it is at all sudden and large enough to cause trauma. It would have a more interesting SCP if we only found a few torn limbs attached. Alas.

Since there is no mention of straps, belts or restrains. I can only assume that the occupant staying in the chair is another (overlooked) property of the chair, or again was likely just due to something else putting it there (and the person hanging onto the chair for deal life after arrival and getting stuck that way after they inflated and decompressed), which would be rather interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That is not what escape velocity means.

item.title: Re: Possible containment update?
item.content:encoded:
Well, obviously, if you apply scientific laws to SCP-1445, it shouldn't do anything, but that applies to pretty much every SCP in the form of a mundane object. The issue at hand is that the Foundation has no clue what SCP-1445, a particular rocket chair suspected to be anomalous, actually does. Based on circumstantial evidence, they think they know, but for all they know it could absolutely nothing or almost anything. That's why Dr. A in the addendum insists on actually testing it out to find out what it does.

Also, reread the Special Containment Procedures.

In the Western world, where references to the object have been found in texts back to 19██ CE, the history of SCP-1445 is to be branded as a myth. References to the object as such have been disseminated into the media, discrediting the actual accounts of the object.

Who's to say that the Mythbusters episode wasn't a part of this disinformation campaign?

item.title: Possible containment update?
item.content:encoded: Back in '04 the Mythbusters tested the actual myth. The thing blew up on the launch-pad, but in-universe, doesn't that A. kinda prove that whatever the Foundation wanted to test, a bunch of 'punks' managed to do on a smaller budget, and B. said 'punks' tried to recreate a SCP?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This seems like a lot of complex writing for a rocket chair.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this mostly for the note at the end. I'm a sucker for getting a sense of the Foundation as a real organisation run by real people with real financial concerns.

item.title: Re: I play too much Kerbal Space Program
item.content:encoded:
Well, that's precisely the issue that I'm attempting to bring up when trying to reconcile that with the object being easily seen by telescope and recovered.

edit: and like a fool, I didn't realize I tacked that post on as a reply to yours. My apologies.

item.title: Re: I play too much Kerbal Space Program
item.content:encoded: I can't speak to where the chair was, but "outside the gravitational field of Earth" rules out any Earth orbit options including the Lagrange points. An orbiting object is by definition being affected by the gravity of the body it is orbiting.

item.title: I play too much Kerbal Space Program
item.content:encoded:
I pretty likey, and maybe I missed elaboration but I'm a little vague about the language used to describe the objects origin point - 'outside the gravitational field of earth.'

Does this mean it is orbiting earth? In which case it is improbable but possible it could be resolved from an earth based telescope. At the risk of getting wholly into you're-no-fun mode I would think it should simply be described as doing just that if that is the case. Maybe as it would be exposed to however many day-night cycles its orbit would amount to and the accompanying temperature differences, it might not make for as clean a preservation for organic bits as one might hope.

Is it orbiting the sun, just outside the earth-moon system, having escaped the lions share of its gravity? In which case it might be made to be visible/detectable at certain periods between very lengthy cycles where the planet and this object more closely converge, and very hard to see given its size and distance.

Perpetually eclipsed by the sun, perhaps resting at L2 Lagrange point? Difficult to detect, but might possibly mean it was stopped or redirected at some point in its accelleration. Or was left at a destination that is no longer there. Hmm!

I like the concept, its just the way the origin point is described seems slightly hand-wavey and makes me a little uncomfortable when reading this, even though I'm little more than a layman when it comes to orbits and what has you.

item.title: Not my thing, but upvoted
item.content:encoded: Not my thing, but I upvoted as a token of respect for the massive research, good writing and positive reception of criticisms. Flying rocket chairs sound silly to me, even knowing that they are a piece of folklore. But good writing is a thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That still doesn't make sense. The gravitational pull of the earth does not actually stop - it extends throughout the universe, just like that of all other masses. Plus, the point at which the earth's gravity becomes negligible (which is what I assume you meant) is pretty far beyond even the Foundation's ability to retrieve it. Depending on you definition of "negligible" that could easily be outside the solar system.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I knew I was falling behind in my readings, but I'm really a month late? I need to pay more attention to dates until I'm caught up. But anyway…

-It's never too late to edit. Judging by the shortness of the "who rated" list, I think you're getting a fair amounto f neutral votes. You might be able to edge them into positive, or even flip that handful of negative votes, if you keep polishing. But that's just my mindset, it's your call.

-It just seems like an unneccessary detail. Not exposing the artifact to open flames would be part of standard preservation, even if it wasn't magical. Especially in containment procedures, I'm firmly of the opinion that every word needs to have a specific reason to be there. Anything that isn't absolutely vital, or can be said in a shorter way, should be cut. But again, just my opinion, there are other schools of thought.

-This seems like a clash of headcanon. I've always just assumed that the Foundation has a staff, a whole department even, of historians and preservationists. With the volume of ancient items/documents that crop up as anomalies, methinks that conservation expertise would be in at least as much demand as physicists. So it was really that they thought it necessary to bring in an outside expert at all that jarred me. Thrice I say, YMMV.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My reply:

I agree that it is a bit too long in some parts, but trying to cut it down would be messy now that it's been up for almost a month already. I will try to keep the length in control in future articles, though, and I'm getting a better sense of what to and what not to do.

The guards searching for incendiaries is just meant to prevent people carelessly bringing a lighter or something like that in. It's not meant to be a protection against maliciousness, just a precaution because even Foundation personnel can have moments of thoughtlessness.

Well, there are some things pointing to the object being special: the hypothesis is meant to indicate that they already have some understanding of it. As for the hired expert, the idea was that A) it was safer for them to assume that it wasn't a chair, and B) being an antique vulnerable to damage, they would want to give it good care to preserve it for the future, should they need it for future study. They would hardly want to lose an SCP to wear-and-tear. You do bring up a valid concern as far as security concerns go, but it works in my headcanon as it's a relatively quiet site and he has very tight clearance restrictions, being allowed to direct the maintenance of this one object and that object only.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A bit long, and not a lot of pay-off for the time investment.
Most of the narrative bits could be cut and replaced with a single sentence.
For example, the story of Mr. C and his involvement could be swapped for "A civilian expert was recruited for authentication and to oversee preservation efforts." or some-such.

Why do the gaurds have to search for incindiaries? I get that fire is a particular hazard here, but you'd think that a simple warning sign would suffice. That line in the containment makes it sound like people actively seek to ignite this unless they are stopped.

I really enjoy the practical decision, it's a characterization of the Foundation we don't often see. But if you're going to invoke that, we have to carry it to it's logical conclusion. As it stands, this is an antique found in a weird spot. It almost certianly got into orbit via anomaly, but there is nothing that actually points to the object itself being special. Why would a budget-conscious administrator bother to hire an expert to work full-time on what could very well be just a chair? That's without even mentioning the security concerns of bringing in a civilian.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow, didn't know how I missed that one. /facepalm

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What I thought was missing was, well, a verb. As in: "the low temperatures it had been at". The rewritten sentence is much clearer. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Both of those are entirely valid criticisms, thanks for pointing them out. I don't think I was missing anything in the first, but I admit that it doesn't read too well so I changed it to actually use the preposition. I'm a bit slow at catching these things: though English is my primary language and I speak it more or less natively, it's still not my first language. That's part of why I write everything so formally, and also part of why I use these weird constructions sometimes. I don't mean to use this to excuse any of my mistakes, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First off, a couple of minor points:

due to the extremely low temperatures it been at

I think you're missing a word there! Also, I'm not sure about the stranded preposition, but I understand it's a point of contention in the English language. :)

At the same time, Mr. C████ had been contacted as an outside expert on his subject and was asked to give an inspection of the object based on his expertise.

This strikes me as redundant. As an expert, he's hardly going to give an inspection based on something other than his expertise.

Other than that, I find the concept solid and I really like the final addendum. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is not without its faults, but STILL.

+1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd be happy to edit it as I already have for the mistakes tretter has pointed out. Although I think that the tone has been significantly improved since it was first put online, I'd be glad to make it a bit better if I knew what exactly you were looking for.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Uhh, whoops. That's completely correct, thanks for catching that. I think I was thinking about the name of the robe at the time, and typed that in for some reason.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just one minor thing, maybe I'm misunderstanding - in the notes:

2. Upon the lighting of all of the fuses, SCP-1445 is capable of reaching escape velocity and generating an anomalous amount of propulsion from the rockets. The hypothesis is supported by the object's final position in space, just outside of Earth's gravitational field. The contemporary description of the event indicated that actual rocketry was involved, not some other as-of-yet unknown method, as the object was referred to as "[…] bringing Han Fu into the aegis of Heaven with a great deal of fire and smoke, to never be seen again."

Shouldn't that be Wan Hu?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, I must of missed the beginning of that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Presumably dying in space wasn't a part of the original plan.

I dig the idea, but the tone is not very good, so I'll abstain from voting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: OK, changed it to "the extremely low temperatures it had been at" (referring to the object itself), which seems like it should be less objectionable. As for getting a lot of sunshine, I don't think that is in and of itself enough to keep it from freezing, though, especially not over the course of more than 600 years.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I want to upvote this article, but space isn't as cold as you'd think. TV Tropes has already written an essay on why better than I could have: http://tvtropes.org/pmwiki/pmwiki.php/Analysis/SpaceIsCold

EDIT: Actually, I'm not sure, but we're pretty near a star, so wouldn't the corpse be getting a LOT of sunshine? Eh, upvoting because it's nifty either way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Could I draw your attention to the description of the examination and the autopsy? Relevant parts are in bold:

Upon detailed examination of the retrieved objects conducted by scientific personnel on site, it was noted that neither the chair nor the body suffered the damage that they should have sustained upon traveling at escape velocity from atmospheric friction and drag. In addition, neither suffered any significant decomposition due to the time spent in the vacuum of space, though the body was frozen solid due to the extremely low temperatures it had spent time in. The body was noted to be wearing a hanfu appropriate to the style of the Ming Dynasty and a wushamao, providing further evidence of the object's time of origin. The clothes were then removed for the autopsy, which determined that the specimen had suffered from brain damage stemming from sudden deceleration, and that the final cause of death was asphyxiation. The specimen also appeared to have been damaged by micrometeroids as the chair had been.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A +1 from me. There are some issues here and there, but they're minor enough for me to upvote still. The site always needs those few SCPs that are one-of-a-kind in some way and stand alone well, but perhaps not in comparison to the rest of the site. It helps it seem more real, the way you had the testing (or lack thereof) handled, and it's something I don't remember seeing in other SCP objects.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like this doesn't address the issue of space effects on the human body. It's probably just me though. No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If only I could +1 discussion posts…

Tone is a definite category that I rate each SCP in. To get that +1, this is something that most certainly has to be taken into account.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's really only room in my heart for one ancient Chinese thing that can into space but shouldn't be, and SCP-907 was much more entertaining than this. It doesn't help that this is pulled more or less directly from mythology.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I find that your comment has plenty of tone errors.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ohh, I see what happened. We parsed the sentence differently. When I read it, I saw it as the recovered objects being examined by scientific personnel, while you parsed it as the recovery done by scientific personnel. I will change that to eliminate ambiguity.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
4. Is using "scientific personnel" to describe researchers really a break in clinical tone?

Yes, because agents on a recovery team will get the artifact, not the researchers.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
OK, I think I understand what you're getting at. Gravitational field will be the term I change it to.

Also, Mr. China would be an amusing name, but it's not. It's an actual Chinese surname.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1. I don't think it's needed, strictly speaking, but it was more of a brief explanation and a nod to practicality, by the Foundation using already existing facilities and resources that required only the addition of a humidifier as per the procedures.
2. Regarding the "sounds like a story thing" - I kind of understand what you're getting at, but I don't think it would seem particularly out of place in a scientific journal or something along those lines. It's simply a description of what was done after recovery, in the same way a scientist might describe what steps were taken preparing for an experiment.
3. Changed it to something other than "he's an expert". Instead he demonstrated the effectiveness of his methods.
4. Is using "scientific personnel" to describe researchers really a break in clinical tone?
5. They're just pointing out that the clothes are appropriate to the alleged time period.
6. Changed that part of the article to make the micrometeroid thing read better.
7. Regenerating fuses: well, that's one of several hypothesized anomalous properties.
8. Addressing the lack of testing and addendum together, seeing as they are related:

Yes, we don't know for sure what this thing does, and yes, it might be an elaborate hoax for all the Foundation knows. That's a major point of this article. The Addendum is a nod to practicality, the Foundation can't really afford to blow millions of dollars on sending a chair to space and then covering it up every single time. Trips to space are expensive in real life and the Foundation realizes it very much. And the second will be rather costly considering how visible a trail of smoke and fire in the air will be, even if it's conducted in a remote condition. And, what if the chair gets damaged/lost? What if it veers off course? The consultations the Site Direction did led him to believe that the benefits of testing would not be worth the massive cost, and all of the risk involved as well. The way he treats the matter is entirely serious: he's pointing out that there are far better things to spend millions of dollars on. Again, it is fairly integral to the article, and I cannot really change it.

In any case, I still thank you for your comments, as they have pointed out some things that I have missed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this, however it's not for the SCP itself(no offense). It just makes the Foundation seem…real. The bureaucratic feel is what clinches it for me, and I love it for seeming so…well, grounded. No pun intended.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconded. I don't really like the object itself, but the fact that you didn't go "lets send some class Ds to space for no reason at all" and actually included a strong argument for why was good. No vote, though. Overall I didn't find it interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Always liked this story. Theres some issues with the article, but I like it as a whole.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the concept, though I found that some sentences in the article seemed a little too wordy (perhaps add a few punctuation marks so the longer sentences don't read as run-on?). Nevertheless, have an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually quite enjoy this. SCPs should have realistic testing logs, even if that means no testing at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it for the most part, particularly for of the Chinese censorship aspect of the containment. But the science in me doesn't like one bit.

just outside the gravitational pull of the Earth

That's just outside the universe… Which I suppose makes this way more anomalous than we first thought.
Quick edit: I really hope Mr. C[BLACKBOX] isn't Mr. China…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Batch #2
here you go

Storage Site-23 was chosen to be the destination of the object after extraterrestrial retrieval, due to general proximity and its standard storage chambers being suitable for its containment.

I dont't know if this is needed.

After it was brought to Storage Site-23, the body was taken away and brought to the medical wing for an autopsy

Now, it sounds like you're telling a story. You know the "And then this happened" kind of story.

At the same time, Mr. C████ had been contacted as an outside expert on his subject and was asked to give an inspection of the object based on his expertise.

See above post.

He had inferred correctly that the piece dated from the Ming Dynasty and noted the damage the object had received, then suggested measures that could restore the object and preserve it in the long-term.

Now, I know SCPs are supposed to tell stories, but not in the same style as you would find in a book.

Based on his knowledge and skill in his field of expertise, he was hired shortly afterwards and inducted as a curator for SCP-1445, and has since led the effort in preserving the object.

You already said he was an expert.

Upon detailed examination of the retrieved objects by scientific personnel, it was noted that neither the chair nor the body suffered the damage that they should have sustained upon traveling at escape velocity from atmospheric friction and drag.

Scientific personnel? Makes me think this: The Foundation personnel run around everywhere bopping people on the head with foam bats, screaming "For Science!"

The body was noted to be wearing a hanfu appropriate to the style of the Ming Dynasty and a wushamao3, providing further evidence of the object's time of origin.

You already said it was from the Ming Dynasty. Why bother repeating yourself?

he specimen also appeared to have suffered from micrometeoroid damage like the chair also had.

Bad tone.

A testing procedure for SCP-1445 has been proposed by Dr. A███, in order to discern the anomalous properties of the object directly through experimentation. After consultation with other departments of the Foundation and a week of consideration, the proposal was denied by Site Director ██████.

Wait… We don't even know what this thing does? For all we know, this may be an elaborate hoax.

The fuses of the object are capable of regeneration. The object was found with fuses whole and unburnt, but contemporary documents clearly describe a "[…] ceremonious lighting of the fuses".

So regenerating fuses? That's only an Anomalous Object, not an SCP.

The addendum.

Yeah, we don't need this. It serves no purpose to the article. It also could be considered a form of LoLFoundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Addressing these concerns in order: (will address other ones when they appear and when I return)

1. In retrospect, "wear-and-tear" does appear to be somewhat out of place. I have changed it to "mishandling", which would probably where most of the damage would probably come from in the first place.
2. "Carbon dated" is an actual way to refer to an item that has been radio-carbon tested.
3. I agree that this is a very bad wording, and it has been changed to something more indicative of their positioning.
4. I used this method of discovery because I had seen it on other SCPs in the past. For example, both SCP-1733 and SCP-261 used similar discovery stories.
5. Reading through it, I think the last sentence was probably the most problematic one. Changed it to "a dead body seated in the chair", which sounds more natural.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Tone errors is a legitimate criticism, though it'd be nice if they were pointed out, especially in large articles like this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Because the object is susceptible to normal wear-and-tear,

There is this.

When carbon dated,

I don't know, maybe it's right.

each of the two horizontal opposite sides

I'm not sure what you are trying to say here.

Foundation personnel monitoring the Internet

"So what do you do at the Foundation?"
"Oh, I browse the Internet all day. And you?"
"I go to amusement parks to look for anomalous stuff."

Finding something over the internet just doesn't sound very professional, IMO.

They were detailed to the point of providing apparent coordinates of [REDACTED] and very low-resolution pictures. The Foundation immediately investigated the rumors with the Foundation telescope located at [REDACTED] and saw the object as well as what appeared to be a dead body in a sitting position.

This doesn't quite sound right.

The errors are scattered here and there. I'll look for more, but I have to go do something quickly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Tone errors are a legitimate criticism.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Or it could be because he legitimately doesn't feel like the voice used in the article is appropriate to a scientific research summary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Tone errors are just one of those dumb non reasons in which a guy goes "I don't like it… but it's not bad. I know! Tone errors!" *downvotes, smugly posts comment*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Could you please elaborate on this? Is the article not adequately clinical in tone? It would help me a lot to know what the specific issue is, so that I can improve it here and in the future. I'm still relatively inexperienced in trying to adopt this kind of tone and it would be good to know where my mistakes were made.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I want to like this, but the tone errors forced me to downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I admit, I was kind of worried about getting this reaction. I generally tried to give the object a kind of "feel" to give it some place in the modern day, with the references to the actual 5 Mao Party and Chinese censorship, as well as use the Addendum to give it some additional draw. I was originally intending on having all sorts of tests for it, but then I realized how much it would cost to retrieve it over and over and all the cover-up required for a simple Safe-class object, and so I used the Addendum to portray the Foundation as being a very busy organization with limited funds and urgent priorities, and the researchers having to make do with making hypotheses that may or may not be true. I completely understand this reaction, though, and as long as you enjoyed the writing itself I'm still quite pleased.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not sure what to think of this. While its very well written, it's essentially one of those "mythical item works exactly as intended" articles. There's no twist or hook other than the historical connection.
